coredata.dc:title: One-pot electrochemical synthesis of carbon dots/TiO2 nanocomposites with excellent visible light photocatalytic activity

coredata.dc:description: We propose a novel electrochemical method to one-pot synthesize carbon dots/titanium dioxide (CDs/TiO2) nanocomposites for the first time. The as-prepared CDs/TiO2 nanocomposites exhibit excellent visible photocatalytic activity for degradation of methylene blue (MB) under a 50W halogen tungsten lamp irradiation.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) XRD patterns, (B) EDX, (C) TEM and (D) HRTEM images of CDs/TiO2 nanocomposites.
FT-IR spectra of (a) CDs, (b) P25, (c) CDs/TiO2 nanocomposites.

(A) MB concentration changes with irradiation time over CDs, P25, uncalcined CDs/TiO2, CDs/TiO2 400, CDs/TiO2 500 and CDs/TiO2 600 under visible light irradiation, respectively. (B) Recyclability of the visible photocatalytic decomposition of MB by CDs/TiO2.
(A) UV–visible DRS spectra of (a) P25, (b) CDs/TiO2. (B) PL spectra of (a) CDs, (b) CDs/TiO2, and (c) P25 under excitation wavelength of 400nm.
The mechanical illustration of enhanced visible photocatalytic activity for CDs/TiO2 nanocomposites.

simple-head.ce:title: One-pot electrochemical synthesis of carbon dots/TiO2 nanocomposites with excellent visible light photocatalytic activity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education), College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan Normal University, Changsha 410081, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education), College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hunan Key Laboratory of Applied Environmental Photocatalysis, Changsha University, Changsha 410022, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hunan Key Laboratory of Applied Environmental Photocatalysis, Changsha University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We propose a novel electrochemical method to one-pot synthesize carbon dots/titanium dioxide (CDs/TiO2) nanocomposites for the first time. The as-prepared CDs/TiO2 nanocomposites exhibit excellent visible photocatalytic activity for degradation of methylene blue (MB) under a 50W halogen tungsten lamp irradiation.

ce:section.ce:para: With the development of industrialized production and increase of population, the problem of water pollution has become increasingly worse. Nowadays, the textile dyeing industry, releasing non-biodegradable organic pollutants into the ecosystem, is a serious threat to the environment. Traditional techniques, such as adsorption, precipitation and reverse osmosis, simply transfer pollutants from one phase to another or concentrate them in one phase instead of actually degrading them [1]. Therefore, it is imperative to develop green and efficient technologies to control and reduce pollution growth. Nanostructured semiconductor photocatalysts are considered to be effective way for wastewater treatment. Among the known semiconductor photocatalysts, titanium dioxide (TiO2) has been used as a popular photocatalyst for environmental purification owing to its good chemical stability, photocatalytic activity, low toxicity, and low cost, etc. [2,3]. However, the relatively large band gap of TiO2 (3.0–3.2eV) limits its application in the visible light region [4]. Therefore, improving the visible photocatalytic activity of TiO2-base photocatalysts for taking full advantage of the solar spectrum is important and significant [5].

A variety of strategies have been utilized to enhance the visible photocatalytic activity of TiO2, such as surface sensitization, semiconductor coupling, metal and non-metal doping [6,7]. Among these, non-metal carbon materials have received considerable attentions because of their availability and non-toxic [8,9]. Conventional carbonaceous materials such as carbon black, graphite and graphitized materials have been widely used in heterogeneous catalysis [10]. Recently, novel carbon nanomaterials such as carbon nanotube, graphene and carbon dots (CDs) have been proven to be excellent photosensitizers which can further enhance photocatalytic activity of TiO2-based composites [11,12]. Among these, the CDs have attracted tremendous interest of scientists due to their unique properties including high chemical stability, low cytotoxicity, excellent biocompatibility, easy functionalization, and good photoinduced electron transfer ability [3].

Several methods have been reported so far for the synthesis of carbon dots/titanium dioxide (CDs/TiO2) nanocomposites, such as the sol-gel process, thermal oxidation and solvothermal synthesis [9,13]. However, most of the synthesis techniques are related to multiple steps: (a) the synthesis of CDs; (b) the synthesis of TiO2; (c) the preparation of CDs/TiO2 nanocomposites. For example, Wang et al. reported a hydrothermal synthesis method for the fabrication of CDs/TiO2 nanocomposites. Firstly, P25 solution was maintained at 150°C for 24h to prepare TiO2 nanowires, and then CDs/TiO2 nanocomposites were synthesized through a mixture of TiO2 nanowires and Vitamin C with another hydrothermal processing at 90°C for 4h [14]. Liu et al. prepared CDs through a modified pyrolysis approach, and the TiO2 nanorod spheres were synthesized by a two-phase hydrothermal method. Then CDs/TiO2 composite with enhanced visible light-driven photocatalytic activity were obtained by a hydrothermal coupling process [15]. Apparently, the multi-step procedures for the preparation of CDs/TiO2 composites are very complex [16]. Therefore, it is of great significance to develop a simple method for one-pot synthesis of CDs/TiO2 nanocomposites.

Herein, we propose a novel electrochemical method to one-pot synthesize CDs/TiO2 nanocomposites for the first time. A possible synthesis mechanism has been discussed. The as-prepared CDs/TiO2 nanocomposites are highly responsive to visible light, which can be used as excellent visible photocatalysts for degradation of methylene blue (MB) under a 50W halogen tungsten lamp irradiation instead of expensive high-intensitive Xenon lamp.
CDs/TiO2 nanocomposites were synthesized by a one-pot electrochemical method. The detailed synthesis procedures are available in the Supporting information.

To obtain highly crystalline CDs/TiO2 nanocomposites, the as-prepared samples were further calcined in an N2 atmosphere [17]. The crystalline structures of CDs/TiO2 nanocomposites were investigated by the X-ray diffraction patterns (XRD). As shown in Fig. 1A, the nanocomposites exhibit strong and sharp characteristic diffraction peaks of rutile phase TiO2 (JCPDS No. 21-1276). No diffraction peaks derived from CDs can be found in XRD pattern, which may be attributed to their poor crystallinity [3]. The energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy was used to identify the elemental composition. The Fig. 1B shows that CDs/TiO2 nanocomposites are composed of C, N, O, and Ti elements. The morphology of the as-prepared CDs/TiO2 nanocomposites was characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Fig. 1C, the TEM image confirms that the size of CDs/TiO2 nanocomposites are 9–35nm, and the average diameter is 17.2nm. It can be seen clearly that some tiny particles are embedded in outer nanoparticles. High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) was further used to reveal the elaborate structure of CDs/TiO2 nanocomposites (Fig. 1D). The clear lattice spacing of 0.22nm in the inner particles is consistent with the (111) facet of rutile TiO2 [18], which agrees well with the XRD results. No obvious lattice fringes are observed in the outer CDs, indicating that the CDs in the as-prepared nanocomposites have an amorphous structure, which is in agreement with the result reported by our group [19] before.

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were collected to further confirm the structure of CDs/TiO2. As shown in Fig. 2, the absorption bands at 3340cm−1 and 1651cm−1 appear in CDs (curve a) are attributed to stretching vibrations of O‒H, N‒H and C=O, respectively [20]. The strong and broad absorption band below 1000cm− 1 of P25 (curve b) is attributed to the combination of the Ti‒O‒Ti vibrations [21]. It can be clearly seen that the FT-IR spectrum of CDs/TiO2 (curve c) exhibits strong and broad absorption bands below 1000cm−1, which indicates the presence of TiO2. Furthermore, the absorption band at 3340cm−1 also appears in CDs/TiO2, which indicates that the successful synthesis of CDs. Moreover, the peak at 1452cm−1 in the FTIR spectrum of CDs/TiO2 is attributed to the coordination between COO− and Ti center [15]. These results indicate that the as-prepared nanocomposites consist of CDs and TiO2.

The CDs/TiO2 nanocomposites have been synthesized successfully by one-pot electrochemical method. The possible mechanism summarized as follows: according to our previous work [19], CDs were synthesized by electrochemical carbonization ethanolamine. During this process, a small amount of water was produced, which made the tetrabutyl titanate hydrolyze to form TiO2. Finally, the nanocomposites were formed through the hybridization of CDs and TiO2.

The visible light photocatalytic activities of CDs/TiO2 nanocomposites were evaluated by monitoring the degradation of MB (1mg/mL) under a 50W halogen tungsten lamp irradiation. According to the previous literature, the photocatalytic activity was influenced by the crystallization [22]. To obtain the optimal visible photocatalysts, the as-prepared samples were further calcined at 400°C, 500°C and 600°C in an N2 atmosphere for 2h, which were labeled as CDs/TiO2 400, CDs/TiO2 500, CDs/TiO2 600, respectively. Firstly, we investigated the stability of MB under the 50W halogen tungsten lamp irradiation. No degradation of MB is observed without the catalyst after 60min of light irradiation, which indicates that MB is stable under these conditions (Fig. S1). Before starting the photocatalytic reaction, the mixtures of MB and photocatalysts were stirring for 1h in the dark to reach the adsorption equilibrium (Fig. S2). Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra of MB after photodegradation by different samples under visible light irradiation are shown in Fig. 3A. There is a negligible degradation of MB under visible light irradiation in the presence of CDs or P25, respectively. However, the photocatalytic degradation efficiency of MB with uncalcined CDs/TiO2, CDs/TiO2 400, CDs/TiO2 500 and CDs/TiO2 600 reaches 27%, 62%, 90% and 71% within 120min, respectively. These results indicate the introduction of CDs have effectively improved the visible photocatalytic activity of TiO2. Compared with the sample before calcination, the calcined CDs/TiO2 nanocomposites both show enhanced visible photocatalytic activity. Among these, the CDs/TiO2 500 has optimal visible light photocatalytic activity. For investigating the stability of CDs/TiO2 nanocomposites, the photocatalytic degradation of MB under visible light irradiation was performed during four cycles. As we can see from Fig. 3B, no obvious decrease of photocatalytic activity is observed after four cycles, which indicates that the as-prepared CDs/TiO2 nanocomposites have good stability.

In order to reveal the functions of CDs for enhanced excellent visible photocatalytic activity of CDs/TiO2 nanocomposites, UV–visible diffuse-reflectance spectra (DRS) and fluorescence (FL) spectra were studied in detail. As shown in Fig. 4A, P25 (curve a) shows strong absorption in the UV region, and no absorption in the visible light range. While CDs/TiO2 (curve b) displays obvious visible light absorption ranging from 400 to 800nm except for strong UV absorption. The results indicate that the CDs can effectively increase the absorption of TiO2 nanoparticles in the visible light region, which is in good agreement with the reports in the literatures [8]. In order to further understand the role of CDs in the nanocomposites, FL spectra of CDs (curve a), CDs/TiO2 (curve b), and P25 (curve c) were comparatively studied. As shown in Fig. 4B, P25 itself has no emission under visible light excitation of 400nm, which indicates there is almost no absorption in the visible light range. CDs display a strong and wide emission peak centered at 450nm, while the FL emission of CDs/TiO2 weakened under the same excitation conditions. The results indicate that effective charge transfer from CDs to TiO2 may take place under visible light irradiation.

Based on the above results and discussion, a possible photocatalytic mechanism for enhanced photocatalytic activity of CDs/TiO2 composites was summarized in Fig. 5. When irradiated under visible light, CDs work as a photosensitizer, and the excited electron can transfer from the conduction band (CB) of CDs to that of TiO2 [15]. The electrons in the CB of TiO2 react with O2 to form •O2 −, and the hole in the surface of CDs will oxidize H2O to form •OH radical, which subsequently both degrade the MB dye.

In summary, we have successfully developed a novel one-step electrochemical method to synthesize CDs/TiO2 nanocomposites. The as-synthesized CDs/TiO2 nanocomposites exhibit excellent visible light photocatalytic activity for the degradation of MB. Mechanistic analysis and characterization results indicate the CDs act as the role of photosensitizers in CDs/TiO2 nanocomposites. Our method possesses of the advantages of simple and low cost, which offers an easy and applicable pathway to obtain excellent photocatalysts for utilizing solar energy more efficiently.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21375037, 21475043), Doctoral Fund of Ministry of Education of China (No: 20134306110006) and Science and Technology Department of Hunan Province (No. 2014FJ6034).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.matlet.2016.03.003.
